definition: The action or process of innovating; a new method, idea, product, etc.
example: Innovation often finds that solving big problems is easier than solving little ones.

definition: The ability to acquire and apply knowledge and skills.
example: We simply do not know what the limit to intelligence is.
source: Comments on AI and future potential

definition: An important assignment carried out for political, religious, or commercial purposes.
example: Our mission is a perfect search engine—one that understands exactly what you mean and gives back exactly what you want.
source: Early Google vision / IPO letter era

example: Don't be evil. Do the right thing. Build products that make the world better.
speech: Don't Be Evil / Do the Right Thing

definition: To suppress or restrain (an action or quality).
A point or level beyond which something does not or may not extend or pass.
example: We simply do not know what the limit to intelligence is.
source: Comments on AI and future potential

definition: A scientific procedure undertaken to make a discovery, test a hypothesis, or demonstrate a known fact.
example: Every experiment multiplies attempts: the only path to success is to allow lots of failures.

definition: The action of one object coming forcibly into contact with another; a marked effect or influence.
example: Impact asks more than “don’t be evil”—we must actively try to be good.
speech: Don't Be Evil / Do the Right Thing

definition: To arrange systematically; to put into a structured whole.
example: The mission was almost absurd in scale: organize the world’s information and make it useful.

definition: Able to be used for a practical purpose; beneficial.
example: Information is not enough—it must become useful in the moment of need.

definition: A strong desire to know or learn something.
example: Hire for curiosity and the product roadmap writes half of itself.

definition: The means or opportunity to approach or use something.
example: Access to knowledge is a quiet form of power redistribution.

definition: The occurrence of valuable discoveries by chance.
example: Great search preserves serendipity—the result you needed but did not know to name.

definition: Lack of secrecy or concealment; willingness to share.
example: Openness in research multiplies progress faster than secrecy multiplies prestige.

definition: A firmly held belief or opinion.
example: Conviction without evidence is noise; conviction after struggle is leadership.

definition: The quality of being coherent and intelligible.
example: Clarity is kindness in speech—confusion is often a way to hide.
